findings.description: SlackBulkNotificationQueueable.execute() iterates over deserialized payloads and calls new Http().send(req) inside the for loop (lines 116-130). Each iteration makes an HTTP callout to Slack_Webhook. With the Salesforce limit of 100 callouts per transaction, processing more than 100 payloads in a single Queueable execution will throw a System.LimitException.
findings.recommendation: Refactor to send one payload per Queueable execution and chain to the next. After processing one payload, re-enqueue with the remaining payloads. Alternatively, batch payloads into groups of up to 100 and process each group in a separate Queueable chain.

findings.description: ServiceNowSyncControlsQueueable.execute() calls syncSingleControl(control) inside a for loop (lines 57-59). The syncSingleControl method (line 280-303) creates an HttpRequest and calls http.send(req), making one callout per control. If a framework has more than 100 controls, this will exceed the callout limit.
findings.recommendation: Batch all controls into a single bulk API call to ServiceNow, or refactor to use a chained Queueable pattern where each execution processes a batch of controls (up to 100) and chains to the next Queueable for the remaining controls.

findings.description: AlertDispatchQueueable.execute() iterates over queueItems (line 224) and calls sendAlertNotificationSync() for each item. The sendAlertNotificationSync method dispatches to sendSlackAlert() (line 373-391) and sendPagerDutyAlert() (line 393-425), both of which make HTTP callouts. If many alert queue items target SLACK or PAGERDUTY channels, this will make multiple callouts within a loop, risking the 100-callout governor limit.
findings.recommendation: Group queue items by channel, batch all Slack payloads into a single combined message or use Slack's bulk API, and likewise for PagerDuty. Alternatively, limit the number of items processed per Queueable execution and chain to the next for remaining items.

findings.description: Query on AI_System_Registry__c at line 138 has no LIMIT clause: [SELECT Name FROM AI_System_Registry__c WITH USER_MODE]. While this object is expected to be low volume, if the subscriber org somehow accumulates thousands of records, this could consume significant query rows. Additionally, lines 262 and 274 query AI_System_Registry__c without LIMIT in the evaluateControl method.
findings.recommendation: Add a LIMIT clause (e.g., LIMIT 1000) to all three queries. If the result set exceeds the limit, log a warning and gracefully handle partial data.

findings.description: In handleFailedAccess() (lines 28-33), parseEventField() is called 3 times per event with different field names, but each call independently deserializes the full JSON payload via JSON.deserializeUntyped(). For N events, this results in 3N JSON deserialization operations. With 200 events in a trigger batch and a complex JSON payload, this creates substantial CPU overhead.
findings.recommendation: Parse the JSON once per event into a Map and pass the Map to a local extraction method. Replace the 3 calls to parseEventField with direct Map.get() calls from the pre-parsed Map.

findings.description: buildCsvContent() at line 362 uses string concatenation (+=) inside a for loop to build the CSV output. Each += creates a new String object, resulting in O(n^2) memory allocation for n gap records. If there are hundreds or thousands of gaps, this causes excessive CPU time and heap consumption.
findings.recommendation: Replace string concatenation with List<String> accumulation and String.join() at the end: collect all CSV lines in a List<String> then return String.join(lines, '\n').

findings.description: buildHtmlReport() uses string concatenation (+=) to build HTML in a loop (lines 315-326, 339-343). The HTML loop is bounded to 10 gap rows (line 314: maxGaps = Math.min(reportData.gaps.size(), 10)) which mitigates the risk, but the topRisks loop (lines 339-343) has no bound.
findings.recommendation: Use List<String>.add() and String.join() for HTML construction. Add a bound on the topRisks loop similar to the gaps loop.

findings.description: ElaroSlackNotifierQueueable implements Queueable but does not use System.attachFinalizer(). While it has try-catch error handling and retry logic via re-enqueueing, an unhandled exception (e.g., platform limit exception, serialization error) that escapes the try-catch will be silently lost. The retry logic inside the catch block would not execute for unhandled exceptions.
findings.recommendation: Implement Finalizer interface or attach a standalone Finalizer class in the execute() method to catch unhandled exceptions and log them. This follows the pattern established by JiraIntegrationQueueable, MultiOrgManagerQueueable, and StepProcessor.

findings.description: ElaroTeamsNotifierQueueable implements Queueable but does not use System.attachFinalizer(). Same issue as GOV-008: an unhandled exception escaping the try-catch would be silently lost without being logged or retried.
findings.recommendation: Implement Finalizer interface or attach a standalone Finalizer class. Follow the same pattern as JiraIntegrationQueueable.

findings.description: ElaroAlertQueueable implements Queueable but does not use System.attachFinalizer(). Unhandled exceptions during alert processing will be lost, potentially leaving compliance alerts undelivered with no error trail.
findings.recommendation: Attach a Finalizer to log unhandled exceptions and optionally re-enqueue for retry.

findings.description: PagerDutyTriggerQueueable and PagerDutyResolveQueueable implement Queueable but do not use System.attachFinalizer(). Unhandled exceptions during PagerDuty incident management will be silently lost.
findings.recommendation: Attach a Finalizer for error logging and retry, following the MultiOrgManagerQueueable pattern.

findings.description: All three ServiceNow Queueable classes (ServiceNowSyncControlsQueueable, ServiceNowPushEvidenceQueueable, ServiceNowSyncStatusQueueable) lack Finalizer implementations. Failed ServiceNow sync operations will have no retry mechanism for unhandled exceptions.
findings.recommendation: Add Finalizer classes with retry logic following the established pattern in JiraIntegrationQueueable.

findings.description: AlertNotificationQueueable and AlertDispatchQueueable (lines 163, 207) implement Queueable but do not attach Finalizers. These classes handle compliance alert delivery, so a silent failure could mean missed critical notifications.
findings.recommendation: Attach Finalizer for error logging. Consider retry logic given the compliance-critical nature of alert delivery.

findings.description: createBellNotification() at line 429 queries CustomNotificationType inside a method that can be called from AlertDispatchQueueable's loop. If multiple queue items target the BELL channel, this SOQL query executes once per BELL item, potentially consuming SOQL limits.
findings.recommendation: Cache the CustomNotificationType query result in a static variable or query it once before the loop and pass it to the method.

findings.description: ConsentExpirationBatch.start() queries Consent__c with only Consent_Given__c = true and Consent_Withdrawn__c = false filters but no date-based filtering. While Database.getQueryLocator can handle up to 50 million rows, the execute() method only logs a count (line 51) without actually processing records, suggesting this batch is not fully implemented.
findings.recommendation: Add a date-based filter (e.g., WHERE Consent_Date__c <= :warningDate) to limit the scope. Complete the implementation to actually identify expiring consents.

findings.description: ElaroHistoricalEventBatch is Database.Stateful and accumulates errorMessages (List<String>) across all batch executions (line 32, 87). If many files fail processing across thousands of batch iterations, this list could grow large enough to approach heap limits in the finish() method.
findings.recommendation: Cap errorMessages to a maximum size (e.g., 100 entries) and add a counter for additional errors not stored. Or write error messages to a custom object instead of accumulating them in-memory.

findings.description: ElaroPDFExporter builds CSV output using string concatenation (+=) inside a loop at lines 202-205. This has the same O(n^2) performance characteristic as GOV-006.
findings.recommendation: Use List<String> accumulation and String.join() pattern for CSV generation.

findings.description: StepProcessor is an exemplary implementation of the Queueable+Finalizer pattern: it uses System.attachFinalizer() (line 39), checks Limits.getQueueableJobs() before chaining (line 48), uses AsyncOptions.setMaximumQueueableStackDepth (lines 103, 115), supports exponential backoff retry via setMinimumQueueableDelayInMinutes (line 104), captures execution metrics (lines 55-64), and publishes Platform Event logs for observability (line 66). This is the gold standard pattern for the codebase.
findings.recommendation: Use this as the reference implementation for all other Queueable classes in the codebase.

findings.description: MultiOrgManagerQueueable properly implements System.attachFinalizer() (line 76), includes retry logic with MAX_RETRIES=2 (line 211), and the MultiOrgManagerFinalizer handles UNHANDLED_EXCEPTION with re-enqueueing. This is a well-implemented pattern.
findings.recommendation: No changes needed. This pattern should be replicated across other integration Queueables.

findings.description: JiraIntegrationQueueable properly implements Finalizer with System.attachFinalizer() (line 55), includes JiraIssueFinalizer with retry logic (line 106), and separates concern between Queueable and Finalizer. Well-structured implementation.
findings.recommendation: No changes needed.

findings.description: ElaroDeliveryQueueable includes a DeliveryFinalizer inner class that implements Finalizer. Follows the established pattern for error recovery.
findings.recommendation: No changes needed.

findings.description: SlackIntegrationQueueable includes a SlackFinalizer inner class that implements Finalizer. Follows the established pattern for error recovery.
findings.recommendation: No changes needed.

findings.description: All 5 triggers (ElaroAlertTrigger, ElaroConsentWithdrawalTrigger, ElaroEventCaptureTrigger, ElaroPCIAccessAlertTrigger, PerformanceAlertEventTrigger) are properly bulkified: they iterate over Trigger.new to collect records, delegate to handler classes, and perform DML/EventBus.publish outside of loops. All use TriggerRecursionGuard for recursion prevention.
findings.recommendation: No changes needed. All triggers follow best practices.

findings.description: CursorStep provides a well-designed abstraction for Database.Cursor-based processing with proper testability via CursorWrapper, bound-respecting fetch operations, and USER_MODE access level enforcement.
findings.recommendation: No changes needed. Other large-dataset processing should leverage this pattern.
